s1_b1: In the teaser, you felt why two masses pull together — space bends, and they roll in. But how HARD do they pull? A pebble and a planet — surely not the same.
s1_b2: So what sets the strength of the pull? If we knew, we could predict the force between any two things in the universe.
s1_b3: It's not a mystery. The pull depends on just two things: how much mass, and how far apart. That's the whole story.
s1_b4: First, the masses. Call them m-one and m-two. More mass, more pull. Double either one, and the force doubles.
s1_b5: Then the distance between them. Call it r. Move them apart, and the pull weakens. But not gently — it drops off fast.
s1_b6: Why so fast? The pull spreads out over a sphere — and a sphere grows with the square of its radius. Double the distance, and the pull drops to a quarter.
s1_b7: One more piece. A number that sets gravity's overall strength — the same everywhere in the universe. Call it big G. It's tiny, which is why you don't feel two coins pull.
s1_b8: Put it together. Force equals big G, times the two masses multiplied, divided by the distance squared. Newton's law of universal gravitation.
s1_b9: What's it for? Hand it any two masses and a distance, and it gives you the exact pull between them — an apple and the Earth, the Earth and the Moon, two galaxies. Not the shape of the path, not why mass bends space — just how strong.
s1_b10: Remember the bend. A steeper bend means a stronger pull — and this equation is exactly how steep. The curve, written as a force.
s1_b11: No more weird codes. m-one and m-two, the two masses. r, how far apart. G, gravity's universal strength. The square, the spreading over a sphere. Read it — that's the pull between anything and anything.
